data.title: FIDU: Industrials Sector 2026 Outlook Is Promising, Upside Is Ahead
data.description:
Despite the threat of economic slowdown and high-interest rates, the US industrials sector delivered a solid performance in fiscal 2025, beating the S&P 500 and MSCI USA indices. The sector is expected to accelerate its growth in 2026 amid the favorable market conditions. The robust US GDP growth rate along with rate cuts is likely to translate into mid-teen percentage earnings growth for the industrials sector. Therefore, I initiate coverage of Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF (FIDU) with a buy rating. My price target for FIDU is $102 per share, implying nearly 20% upside from the current level.

The Industrials Sector Growth Is Expected To Accelerate in 2026

The US industrials sector outshone the S&P 500 index and MSCI USA Index in fiscal 2025 as stronger than expected economic growth supported the sector’s performance. In addition, an unprecedented demand in the aerospace & defense industry contributed significantly to the sector’s uptrend. Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF delivered nearly 19% in total return, comprising 17% in price and 2% in dividend return.

I expect FIDU to deliver nearly 20% in price return and 22% in annual total return in 2026. My price target and total return estimates are in line with its three-year annual average. In the past three years, FIDU generated a total return of 68%. I attribute my price target estimate to the economic trends, easing monetary policy, and robust earnings growth power. In fact, the fund could surpass its annual average because of the more favorable conditions in 2026 compared to the past few years.

The Industrials is a cyclical sector, as its performance is significantly correlated to the broader economic conditions and rate policies. The economic expansion increases demand for industrials products and services, while contraction can negatively impact its performance.

Favorable Market Conditions and Earnings Growth Power

In the last few years, the US economic growth was resilient despite multiple risks, growing around 2.5% in 2023 and 2.8% in 2024. The US GDP is expected to increase 3% in 2025. In the past two quarters, the growth was higher than expected, increasing around 3.8% in the second quarter of 2025 and 4.3% in the September quarter. The Atlanta Fed expects the final quarter GDP growth to be around 2.7%.

In fiscal 2026, the US economy is expected to experience a positive impact from rate cuts. A low-rate environment generally helps in increasing business activities and raising consumers spending power. The Fed rate is currently hovering in a range of 3.50% to 3.75%, down from the recent peak of 5.50% to 5.75%. The market currently expects two more rate cuts in 2026 to end the year around 3.0% to 3.25%. In addition, the risk of tariffs has also declined compared to 2025 as countries across the world seek to adjust trade policies through negotiations.

In the September quarter, the industrials earnings growth of 15.7% year over year topped the consensus of 7.9%. The sector's earnings growth was driven by the defense & aerospace, ground transportation, electrical equipment, and construction & engineering industries. Moreover, the 2026 outlook also appears robust amid the consensus earnings growth forecast of 15%.

FIDU’s Diversified Portfolio Offers Broader Industrials Sector Exposure

The Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF offers one of the best ways to gain significant access to the US industrials sector. The fund tracks the MSCI USA IMI Industrials 25/25 Index by using a representative sampling technique. According to FIDU's fact sheet, the fund’s portfolio is spread across 26 industries within the industrials sector.

It offers a higher weight in the aerospace & defense, industrial machinery & supplies & components, electrical components & equipment, construction machinery & heavy transportation equipment, building products, and others.

FIDU’s portfolio includes large, mid, and small caps. Its portfolio comprises 363 companies, with the top 10 making-up 30%. Reviewing its top 10 holdings can also help us gauge the impact of favorable market conditions on the industrials sector.

For example, GE Aerospace (GE) is its largest stock holding. The company’s share price rallied 87% in the last twelve months, supported by a strong demand for aerospace products. In the September quarter, its revenue grew 26%, while earnings per share increased by 44% to $1.66. Furthermore, the company's free cash flow conversion rate was around 130%. It also lifted its full-year forecast, now expecting revenue growth in the high teens to low 20s range compared to its previous guidance of mid-teen percentage growth.

RTX Corp. (RTX), a defense and aerospace company, also delivered an exceptional price gain of 61% in the last twelve months. It topped the September quarter estimate and raised its full-year adjusted sales outlook to the range of $86.5 - $87.0 billion, up from the previous forecast of $84.75 - $85.5 billion. Its new adjusted earnings per share outlook of $6.10 - $6.20 is higher than the previous forecast of $5.80 - $5.95.

After years of internal and regulatory challenges, The Boeing Company (BA) also begins to shine. The commercial aircraft maker’s stock price rallied 32% in the last twelve months. Its backlog grew to $636 billion, including over 5,900 commercial airplanes.

Besides the defense and aerospace, FIDU’s other top holdings also contributed to its performance. For example, Uber Technologies (UBER), a passenger transporter, saw a share price rally of 30% year-to-date amid its strong revenue and earnings growth. The company expects a 17% to 21% boost in full-year gross bookings to $52.25B to $53.75B with adjusted EBITDA growing around 31% to 36%.

GE Vernova (GEV), a heavy electric equipment maker, experienced a price gain of 80% in the last twelve months. Its orders grew 55% year over year to $14.6 billion in the September quarter, enlarging the total backlog to $135 billion. In the September quarter, its revenue increased 10% with the double-digit growth in both equipment and services segments. Its portfolio includes numerous other prominent companies, such as Parker Hannifin (PH), 3M Co. (MMM), General Dynamics (GD) and Emerson Electric (EMR).

FIDU’s other interesting characteristics include a low expense ratio of 0.08%. In addition, the fund offers a dividend yield of around 1%. It has paid dividends in the past 12 consecutive years. Moreover, FIDU’s valuations are in-line with the broader market indices. Its trailing PE is around 27x, while its price-to-book ratio is close to 5.5. Although FIDU and the broader market's valuations are higher compared to their 5- and 10 years averages, I believe a risk of valuation burst is limited unless accompanied by other macro factors.

Risk Factors

The industrial sector can be hit harder in the case of a tariff-related war. According to the FactSet data, the term 'tariffs' was cited in 51 earnings calls of the Industrials sector. The Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF can also respond to economic trends and interest rate policies. Its standard deviation of 12% and annualized volatility of 20% are higher than the median of all ETFs. Overall, the ETF carries an aggressive risk factor.

In Conclusion

The industrials sector’s boom is expected to intensify in 2026 as economic growth and interest rate policies favor cyclical businesses. A mid-teen earnings growth forecast for fiscal 2026 also indicates a solid growth trend. Therefore, the sector offers an opportunity for investors to earn healthy returns.

Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF could be a worthwhile investment vehicle. FIDU’s portfolio exposure to large, mid, and small caps from numerous industries enables it to capitalize on bullish market conditions. Its low expense ratio, above-average liquidity, and potential to pay a decent quarterly dividend make it an interesting candidate.

data.title: The Supreme Court's Review Of IEEPA Tariffs: Why It Matters To The Gold Market (Or Does It?)
data.description:
The gold market faced significant developments throughout 2025 as participants responded to evolving drivers, including US tariff policies. After considerable uncertainty, the White House1 clarified that gold imported for investment purposes would be exempt from tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA)2. However, the story may not end with this exemption. The Supreme Court’s ongoing review of the Trump administration’s application of IEEPA tariffs3 will have broad implications for commodity markets, especially gold. This blog, prepared in collaboration with Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck, examines the legal context, potential effects on commodity prices, and the specific ramifications for gold as market participants, investors, and policymakers prepare for possible changes to the current tariff regime.

Legal and constitutional context

At the heart of the Supreme Court’s deliberations are two pivotal questions: Can Congress delegate to the President the authority to impose tariffs as expansively as has been done under IEEPA? And did Congress do so in the language of IEEPA? These questions reflect a deeper constitutional tension between the explicit power of Congress to regulate tariffs and taxes – a core legislative function – and the President’s broad authority over foreign affairs, which has historically included significant latitude in responding to international threats and emergencies.

During oral arguments, justices grappled with the meaning of “regulate importation” as used in IEEPA. Some pressed the administration to provide statutory examples of where “regulate” equates to “tax”, while others considered whether the statute’s language could be interpreted to allow licensing regimes rather than direct tariff imposition. This textual debate is critical, as a decision that “regulate” does not include the power to levy tariffs would allow the Court to resolve the case without delving into more complex constitutional doctrines.

Beyond statutory interpretation, the Court’s deliberations have been shaped by two key constitutional principles: the major questions doctrine and the non-delegation doctrine. The major questions doctrine holds that Congress must speak clearly when delegating authority over issues of vast economic or political significance. Given the scale of the tariffs in question – impacting trillions of dollars in trade – the challengers argue that IEEPA’s text is insufficiently explicit to support such a delegation. The non-delegation doctrine, meanwhile, restricts the ability of Congress to transfer its core powers to the executive branch without clear standards. The administration contends that IEEPA provides adequate guidance, especially in the context of national security and foreign policy. However, the justices have raised hypotheticals – such as the imposition of a 50% tariff on gas-powered cars in response to a climate emergency – to test the limits of the administration’s theory and the potential for unchecked executive action.

Practical and economic implications

The stakes of the Supreme Court’s decision extend far beyond legal theory. The tariffs imposed under IEEPA have generated billions of dollars in revenue4 and have had a significant impact on global trade relationships. If the Court invalidates these tariffs, many companies will likely seek refunds, creating substantial administrative and financial challenges for the government. Moreover, the ruling could alter the dynamics of trade negotiations, as the threat of swift, sweeping tariffs has been a powerful tool in bringing other countries to the bargaining table.

Notably, certain tariffs – such as those imposed under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act 1962 and Section 301 of the Trade Act 1974 – would remain unaffected by the Court’s decision. These authorities provide alternative mechanisms for the administration to pursue its trade and national security objectives, albeit with more targeted and procedurally defined tools.

Options if IEEPA is overturned

Should the Supreme Court strike down the use of IEEPA for tariff imposition, the administration has several alternative statutory tools at its disposal:

  • Section 232 (Trade Expansion Act 1962): Allows the President to impose tariffs on imports that threaten national security, following an investigation by the Department of Commerce. While effective for sector-specific actions, it cannot easily support broad, across-the-board tariffs.
  • Section 301 (Trade Act 1974): Empowers the US Trade Representative to investigate and remedy unfair trade practices, including through tariffs. These actions are country-specific where there is evidence of trade violations, offering flexibility but not universal coverage.
  • Section 338 (Tariff Act 1930): Enables the President to impose additional tariffs on imports from countries that discriminate against US commerce. Its lack of precedent makes it a risky but potentially powerful tool.
  • Section 122 (Trade Act 1974): Allows for the imposition of temporary import surcharges or quotas to address serious trade deficits, subject to caps of 15% and 150 days. It could serve as an interim measure while longer investigations are conducted.

Together, these authorities form a “plan B” for maintaining tariff leverage. The administration could deploy Section 232 for strategic sectors, use Section 301 to target specific countries and practices, explore Section 338 for counter-discrimination, and activate Section 122 for short-term coverage. However, none of these statutes permit the kind of sweeping, universal tariffs attempted under IEEPA.

Impact on gold prices

Gold’s unique role

Gold has historically benefited from periods of trade policy uncertainty and tariff-driven market volatility. During the recent tariff regime, gold prices surged to record highs, driven by safe-haven flows, central bank buying, and investor diversification. The threat and implementation of tariffs led to significant changes in gold market behaviour. COMEX gold inventories rose sharply as traders moved physical gold from London to New York, anticipating potential disruptions, and US gold premiums were driven up relative to global prices.

If tariffs are overturned

The removal of tariffs will likely raise questions on all assets, commodities and even potentially gold. For gold, this could lead to increased volatility, regional price impact and the movement of large stocks to and from the U.S. Gold may experience a short-term pullback as uncertainty fades and risk appetite returns to other asset classes. However, elevated geoeconomic risks and ongoing policy volatility are more likely to support gold prices.

Over the longer term, gold’s spot market has remained resilient and well-behaved, generally benefiting from flight-to-quality flows. Even if tariffs are overturned, gold is expected to remain supported by central bank demand, diversification needs, and its role as a hedge against broader economic and policy risks.

Considerations for gold market participants

Overall, the gold market’s reaction to tariff developments has been more pronounced in physical flows and regional premiums than in spot prices – a reminder that trade policy can reshape market mechanics even when headline prices remain resilient. If tariffs are overturned, we are likely to see the physical market experience an increased level of activity between the London OTC market and the NY market, with physical stocks moving aggressively to address the condition. There is also the potential for the market to assess and move quickly to navigate refund litigation and customs procedures, while staying attentive to the possibility that new tariff authorities could reintroduce volatility. All the while, gold’s safe-haven appeal is expected to remain intact, supported by persistent geopolitical and economic uncertainty, making ongoing monitoring of global risk factors essential for market participants.

Prepare for the pivot

The Supreme Court’s decision on IEEPA tariffs will have profound implications for the future of US trade policy, the balance of power between Congress and the President, and the practical realities of global commerce. For some commodity markets, the removal of tariffs could exert downward pressure on prices, though the adjustment will likely be gradual and uneven, reflecting the complexities of supply chains and contract renegotiations. For gold, the story is more nuanced: while tariff-driven uncertainty may have contributed to a higher gold price, its role as a safe haven and hedge against broader risks ensures continued resilience, even after regional premiums normalized and trading patterns rebalanced.

As the legal and economic landscape evolves, stakeholders must remain vigilant, adaptive, and prepared for a new era in the regulation of international trade. The persistence of Section 232 and 301 authorities ensures that tariffs will remain a central feature of US trade policy, albeit in more targeted forms. Businesses should prepare for potential refund claims, shifts in exposure from universal tariffs to sectoral or country-specific actions, and ongoing policy volatility as new investigations and proclamations emerge.

The Supreme Court’s ruling will shape the contours of executive action and legislative oversight for years to come. Whether the outcome brings immediate relief or ushers in a new phase of market volatility, the gold market and broader commodity sectors will continue to play a critical role in reflecting and responding to the evolving dynamics of global trade and policy risk.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 2 Mega Dividends Designed To Reward Loyalty; Yields +7.5%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Are you an avid collector of frequent flyer miles when you travel or shop? In May 1981, American Airlines’ AAdvantage was launched, forming the basis of the first modern travel rewards program, where customers could earn miles, which could be used towards free flights and upgrades. Since then, these programs have evolved so much, and major banks are now their core partners.

The truth is that these frequent flyer programs were never just about free flights. These are carefully engineered to reward loyalty, influence behavior, and make travelers choose one group of airlines. Today, these programs aren’t just about miles flown. It is about how much you paid for your ticket, the cabin class you chose, and your existing status in the program. The real rewards often flow to those who readily spend on co-branded credit cards, generating lucrative interchange revenue for airlines and banks.

The program incentives encourage customers to stay engaged, spend more, and remain loyal over long periods of time. The airlines win, the banks win, social media influencers win, and some disciplined customers who understand the rules of the game stand to benefit.

It works very similarly in the stock market. Companies that consistently reward shareholders with dividends encourage long-term ownership. Those dividends, when reinvested, buy more shares, and those shares generate even more dividends. This creates a powerful compounding effect over time, with the biggest beneficiaries being those who invest greater amounts and those who invest more consistently. Just like how frequent flyers benefit from sticking to a specific program, patient investors are often rewarded for staying the course with income investing.

With that framework in mind, let’s look at two companies that have rewarded shareholder loyalty for years.

Pick No. 1: MO – Yield 7.7%

For over 60 years, smoking has consistently been thought of as a thing of the past. It began in 1964, with the U.S. Surgeon General’s report linking cigarettes with dangerous health effects like lung cancer and heart disease. This was believed to be the end of cigarettes. Then, in the '80s, we saw bans imposed on indoor smoking and heavy restrictions on advertising. A company forced to advertise against its own product – now that puts the product in terminal decline, right?

In 1998, the Master Settlement Agreement towards resolving tobacco lawsuits led to strict marketing limits, and companies had to fund public health initiatives. This brought in serious controls on tobacco products in the United States and was surely the end of cigarettes, right? In the 2000s, public health advocates and analysts boldly predicted that smoking would disappear by 2020, creating smoke-free generations ahead.

Here we are, in January 2026, where combustible cigarettes are not only relevant but also significant among adults in America. Moreover, Big Tobacco companies have expanded into product categories that are more appealing to younger generations, with rapidly growing sales and expanding market position.

Tobacco products are highly taxed, and manufacturers raise prices without much impact on overall demand. Looking at the November CPI report, tobacco and smoking products experienced a 6.9% YoY price increase, with cigarette prices in particular soaring 8.2% YoY.

Altria Group, Inc. (MO) is one of the largest producers and marketers of tobacco products globally. We have regularly discussed MO and our other tobacco favorite – British American Tobacco (BTI). Just like the market’s outlook for the tobacco industry since the 60s, our articles were met with skepticism. Yet, MO and BTI have delivered strong performance over the past five years.

MO specifically has experienced a sell-off following the Q3 earnings, and we welcome yet another opportunity to add to our position in this Dividend King.

Altria maintains an investment-grade BBB+ rated balance sheet with $3.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents at the end of Q3. The company’s leverage currently stands at 2x Adjusted EBITDA, and MO raised its full-year Adjusted EPS guidance to $5.37-$5.45. MO also delivered a 3.9% YoY increase to its quarterly dividend, and its new $1.06/share stands strong at a 78% payout ratio. With 56 years of annual dividend increases, MO has returned ~$6 billion to shareholders in the first nine months of 2025, including $5.2 billion in dividends and $712 million in share repurchases. The company has expanded its repurchase program to $2 billion and expects mid-single-digit dividend growth annually through 2028.

Altria owns iconic combustible brands with growing operating margins, and the company continues to utilize these profits to grow its oral and smokeless products, like On! Plus, Ploom and SWIC. While Altria lags with emerging products when compared with BTI’s Vuse, the company has secured FDA marketing approval for its On! Plus nicotine pouches, and is currently seeking a similar approval for Ploom and Marlboro HeatSticks.

There is much to look forward to from MO in 2026 and beyond, and we will do that while enjoying growing dividends and a 7.7% QDI yield at current prices.

Pick No. 2: KRP – Yield 12.5%

Oil has had a rough 2025, with WTI down 20% and the IEA (International Energy Agency) forecasting a massive 3.84 million barrels per day supply surplus next year. This has the markets getting very bearish on oil, and with it, companies will focus on efficiency from existing wells rather than aggressive expansion. However, with natural gas, drilling activity is expected to rise, driven by LNG export demand. As LNG export capacity ramps up, operators will keep rigs running to feed the terminals, effectively putting a floor under domestic production volumes regardless of the bearish crude macro.

Kimbell Royalty Partners LP (KRP) will be a beneficiary of this environment, given its greater exposure to natural gas (48% of the operating mix as of Q3 2025). KRP owns mineral royalty interests in key basins, and its Haynesville interests (representing 9% of KRP’s total rig inventory) are 93% natural gas, followed by Appalachia (87%) and Mid-Continental (56%) basins. This helps keep production levels resilient against downswings in oil prices.

“We're seeing most of our operators indicating that they want to keep production relatively flat.” – Davis Ravnaas, President & CFO

When asked about 1H 2026 production relative to the 2025 guidance, the CFO said

“I'd say flat to increasing. We see no indication of production on our properties falling.” – Davis Ravnaas, President & CFO

With a substantial natural gas and NGL weighting, KRP captures the upside of this export-driven activity, while its royalty structure insulates it from operating costs that E&P firms bear.

Note: KRP is a 1099-issuing C-Corp for tax purposes, and the company estimates that about 93% of its 2025 distributions are expected to be considered a return of capital (similar to 2024), which can offer significant tax deferral advantages.

KRP has maintained its average daily run-rate production guidance for 2025, and per the earlier remarks, the expectation for 2026 is flat to modest increases. This bodes well for the dividend payments in 2026. As a reminder, KRP maintains a policy of distributing 75% of its DCF (distributable cash flow) to shareholders in the form of dividend payments, and since DCF is determined by production volumes and commodity prices, it is a variable payer. The company’s Q4 dividend of $0.35/share calculates to a 12.5% annualized yield. Since its IPO in 2017, KRP has returned ~73% of its IPO price in the form of cash distributions, and the company has a lot more to distribute in the years ahead. Source

KRP recently amended its $625 million credit facility and extended the duration to 2030, with management noting a reduction in the pricing grid and the removal of a credit spread adjustment, which lowered the borrowing cost. The max size of KRP’s credit facility has been increased to $1.5 billion from $750 million, providing greater financial flexibility. We know KRP has been a serial acquirer of royalty acreage when the markets are pessimistic, and the expanded credit facility suggests that the company is preparing for opportunities.

Conclusion

Frequent flyer programs favor consistency over occasional activity. Similarly, dividend investing rewards patience, discipline, and long-term commitment. The true value doesn’t come from a single payout but from the cumulative effect of reinvested dividends compounding over time, much like those miles quietly stacking up flight after flight.

Both Kimbell Royalty Partners and Altria have demonstrated a strong commitment to rewarding their shareholders through steady, repeatable distributions that encourage investors to remain engaged year after year. These businesses value loyalty and are willing to share the benefits with those who stick around.

In investing, as in travel, the biggest rewards often go to those who choose wisely, stay consistent, and let time do the heavy lifting. This is the beauty of our Income Method at High Dividend Opportunities, and the power of income investing.

data.title: U.S. And Canada Jobs Day And More
data.description:
The US dollar (DXY) enjoys a firmer day today ahead of what promises to be a memorable North American session that features the US December employment report and the Supreme Court’s judgement on the legality of the widespread tariffs implemented under the president’s emergency powers. The larger-than-expected rise in household spending in Japan and German industrial output appeared to have little impact. The greenback is trading at new highs against both the euro and the yen. The US employment data is not expected to have deteriorated, which will reinforce ideas that the Federal Reserve is on hold until at least Q2. Meanwhile, for some time, many have expected the use of the president’s emergency powers to be struck down by the high court. Late yesterday, President Trump appeared to order Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to buy $200 bln in mortgages in an attempt to drive down rates. There seemed to be little immediate impact, and to the extent that some economists see an impact, it is limited to 25-50 bp. The average 30-year mortgage rate is about 6.15%, the lowest since October 2024.

Prices

G10

• The euro remains on the backfoot despite stronger-than-expected Germany industrial production. The single currency was sold to a new low for the week near $1.1635 as the market positions for today’s US jobs data. The next target is the retracement objective closer to $1.1610.

• The expiration of large options in the last two sessions at JPY157 and the poor labor earnings data cleared the way for the dollar’s advance today to almost JPY157.75. It was undeterred by the stronger-than-expected rise in household spending. The dollar’s highs in November and December last year were around JPY157.70-90. There are about $773 mln in options struck at JPY158 that expire today and another set for almost $950 mln that expires Monday.

• Sterling is no match for the dollar today. It is testing support at $1.3400, which held on New Year’s Eve. The 200-day moving average is slightly lower (~$1.3395), and the next retracement target is near $1.3365. Options for around GBP510 mln at $1.3350 expire today.

• The US dollar reached almost CAD1.3890 yesterday and is consolidating slightly below there today ahead for the US and Canadian employment data. The Canadian dollar typically holds up better on the crosses in a firm US dollar environment. A move above CAD1.3900 targets CAD1.3945. There are $720 mln of options at CAD1.3880 and another stack for nearly $635 mln at CAD1.3900 that expires today.

• The Australian dollar peaked on Wednesday near $0.6765 before reversing lower. The losses have been extended to about $0.6675 today. Monday’s low is the next technical target, a little below $0.6665. The 20-day moving average is about $0.6680, and the Aussie has not closed below it since late November.

EM

• The greenback is threatening to break out of the consolidative range against the Mexican peso. A move above the MXN18.03-04 area could spur US dollar gains into the MXN18.10-13 area.

• The PBOC fixed the dollar lower today after lifting the reference rate for two consecutive sessions on Wednesday and Thursday. It was set at CNY7.0128 today, compared with CNY7.0288 at the end of last year. Against the offshore yuan, the dollar held barely above this week’s low seen Monday near CNH6.9780.

• The dollar recovered from yesterday’s low near INR89.7365 to INR90.2525 today. The week’s high was INR90.2925. News that the central bank reserves fell by $9.8 bln in the past week, the largest decline since November 2024, reflects the stepped-up effort to steady the exchange rate.

Other Markets

• Equities are mixed today. Most of the large Asia-Pacific bourses rallied today, with the notable exception of Taiwan, Australia, and India. Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) is up about 0.5%, which, if sustained, recoups the losses of the past two sessions. US index futures (SPX) are firm.

• While JGBs softened and the 10-year yield rose almost two basis points, benchmark yields in Europe are narrowly mixed. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is up a couple of basis points to almost 4.19%.

• Gold is consolidating between about $4453 and $4485. The week’s high was slightly above $4500.

• February WTI has so far today been confined to the upper end of yesterday’s range. Today’s range is about $57.60-58.55.

Data

  • As the week progressed, economists became more confident of a firm, even if subdued, increase in US nonfarm payrolls. The median in Bloomberg’s survey now stands at 70k (up from 45k a week ago), with earnings expected to tick up and the unemployment rate to slip. If true, it will reinforce ideas that like last year, the Federal Reserve will begin this year with an extended pause following three rate cuts. That said, job growth has stalled, especially given Fed Chair Powell’s admission that the monthly figure may be overstated by around 60k. Assuming the median projection is correct, the three-month average increase in nonfarm payrolls would be about 10k. The reaction to the jobs data may be more subdued than usual as participants await the Supreme Court’s long-awaited verdict on the president’s use of emergency powers to levy broad tariffs. Although there are other ways to secure high tariffs, most measures require a greater role for Congress.
  • Canada also reports on the December labor market. After employment jumped by 53.6k in November (all part-time positions, while losing 9.4k full-time jobs), a small pullback is likely in December. The unemployment rate peaked last August and September at 7.10% and fell to 6.5% in November (from 6.9% in October). The risk is that the unemployment rate rose in December (6.7%) and the participation rate likely increased. Wage growth may ease slightly to 3.8% from nearly 4% in October and November, which matched the 2025 high. It seems too early to re-evaluate ideas that the Bank of Canada is on hold through at least the first half of the year.
  • Mexico reported slightly lower December inflation than expected, but at 3.69% and 4.33% for the headline and core rates, respectively, it did not appear to impact policy expectations. Banxico is on hold for the next several months, at least. Today’s attention shifts to November industrial production. After falling from June through September, Mexico’s industrial output rose by nearly 0.75% in October and is expected to have crept up in November.
  • The eurozone reported a 0.2% rise in November retail sales after October’s flat showing was revised to a 0.3% increase, consumption rose 1.1% in Q3 - its weakest showing since Q2 '24 - and it appears to be off to a firmer start to Q4 '25, but earlier today, France reported a 0.3% decline in consumer spending in November (0.5% in October) for a flat performance year-over-year. Separately, following on the heels of the surge in factory order reported yesterday, Germany announced a 0.8% increase in November industrial output, following a 2.0% jump in October (revised from 1.8%). France reports its industrial output slipped 0.1% in November.
  • Japanese household spending surged 2.9% year-over-year in November, nearly offsetting in full the 3% decline in October. Yesterday’s Japan reported disappointing wage data, and the swaps market has reduced the amount of tightening discounted this year from about 48.5 bp on Monday and Tuesday to about 40 bp now, the lowest since Christmas.
  • China reported that the December CPI rose 0.8% year-over-year (0.7% in November), which is the highest since February 2023. Many had attributed the deflation seen in half the months in 2024 to weak consumption, yet the rise in consumer prices in recent months does not appear to have coincided with a rise in consumption. Producer price deflation moderated to -1.9% from -2.2% in November. It is the least producer price deflation since August 2024 and may reflect the anti-involution campaign.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: MoonLake: Positive FDA Meeting Spurs Momentum Ahead Of BLA Submission
data.description:
Thesis

As you know, MoonLake Immunotherapeutics (MLTX) stock had a pretty rough 2025. And we're only now starting to see it recover from the lows of the $6 range we saw it drop to in late September. It seems all the positives were priced out of the sonelokimab back then, but with yesterday's announcement, the stock is starting to show momentum again. The outcome from the Type B meeting with the FDA is that MoonLake can potentially establish substantial evidence of effectiveness without actually conducting any additional trials. It's good news for investors as it brings the timeline for sonelokimab much closer, and we could see a BLA in 2H26. In terms of stock price, I don't think it's too late to buy into MoonLake now, with still a lot of time to go until the BLA. We have earnings coming up next month, and I think management's outlook will be quite bullish following the news this week. There could be a lot of upside to this stock in 2026.

3Q25

Back in 3Q25, MoonLake reported a net loss of about $70.7 million, up a bit from the $56.1 million loss we saw in 2Q25. It's a continuation of investment in expanding their clinical development programs. As you would expect, R&D expenses increased, up now to about $60.6 million from $49.8 million the previous quarter. This was mainly driven by higher CRO/CMO, consulting, and personnel costs to support their late-stage trials and, most importantly, the BLA preparations for sonelokimab. G&A expenses, on the other hand, remained quite stable at just $10.8 million. In terms of cash, the company held a hefty position of $380.5 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term marketable securities. Now, management has told us they expect this sum to fund operations into the 2H27. This estimate also makes sense when you take the 3Q25 operating loss of $71 million as a figure for cash burn. Based on that cash sum, they should have funds for operations over the next 15 to 17 months.

The pipeline is sonelokimab

To touch just briefly on their pipeline, as you know, sonelokimab is MoonLake’s only clinical-stage drug candidate. So much of their operations centres on sonelokimab, which is why the stock can see such positive swings on the back of good news. The candidate itself is a Nanobody targeting IL-17A/F for inflammatory diseases and is undergoing quite a few key programs, including the likes of Phase 3 VELA-1/VELA-2 in adult hidradenitis suppurativa (HS), in which we’re seeing ongoing long-term data. They also have the VELA-TEEN trial in adolescent HS and two Phase 3 IZAR trials in psoriatic arthritis (PsA). Additional studies surrounding sonelokimab would include the Phase 2 LEDA trial in palmoplantar pustulosis and the Phase 2 S-OLARIS trial in axial spondyloarthritis. On the back of the news we saw yesterday, the HS and PsA programs would be nearest to potential approval. Since sonelokimab for HS is close to a BLA in 2H26, I made a table to summarise the studies/data we have seen so far, as this will be critical in understanding the FDA viewpoint from the Type B meeting.

Positive Outcome from Type B Meeting

As you probably heard, MoonLake yesterday announced some very highly positive regulatory outcomes from its Type B meeting with the FDA. What we know now is that the FDA has given them clear validation of its approval strategy for sonelokimab in HS. And most importantly, the FDA confirmed that MoonLake can establish substantial evidence of effectiveness without actually conducting any additional HS trials. It means that they can support a BLA based on the already existing data from the Phase 2 MIRA trial and Phase 3 VELA-1 and VELA-2 trials. Now, this is great news; the FDA clearly sees a case here, and one that can result in a positive outcome. MoonLake does not need to burn cash on additional studies, and it brings the whole timeline for sonelokimab much closer.

Inclusion of MIRA data

If we combine the headcount for the Phase 2 MIRA trial and Phase 3 VELA-1 and VELA-2 trials, together they enrolled more than 1,000 HS patients, which should be satisfactory. However, the FDA specifically advised for the inclusion of MIRA data to support efficacy and VELA-2 data to inform the overall safety profile, even though VELA-2 narrowly missed statistical significance under one analysis approach due to an unusually high placebo response. Now, I think this point from the FDA is more about how they view the totality of evidence rather than judging each trial in isolation.

As a quick recap, MIRA was MoonLake’s Phase 2, randomised, placebo-controlled clinical trial of sonelokimab. It was done in adult HS and represents the foundational efficacy study for the program going forward. Specifically in the trial, MIRA was about evaluating subcutaneous sonelokimab at 120 mg and 240 mg against a placebo. Now, HiSCR75, so a ≥75% reduction in abscess and inflammatory nodule count was set as the primary endpoint, and this actually made it the first placebo-controlled HS trial to use this much higher bar endpoint. The trial showed us a pretty robust and statistically significant efficacy, with a 43% HiSCR75 response rate for the 120 mg sonelokimab outcome at week 12, and also about a 29 percentage-point improvement over the placebo. We also saw a favourable safety profile alongside this. So if you think about the MIRA trial in Phase 2, the FDA wants it since it clearly met its primary endpoint with a pretty large, statistically significant treatment effect. Now, even though this is just a Phase 2 study, MIRA was very well-controlled, randomised, and even used the same high bar endpoint in HiSCR75 as VELA. So, as a result, I think the FDA considers MIRA relevant and credible evidence of efficacy that can actually contribute to establishing the substantial evidence of effectiveness they want when combined with Phase 3 data.

Removing an overhang

Now, another reason why we saw such a big 27% stock jump is that this feedback from the FDA essentially removes a major regulatory overhang. You see, many analysts were pretty doubtful about MoonLake's regulatory timeline and thought that the FDA would need additional Phase 3 trials or another new study to demonstrate efficacy. There was a hanging uncertainty over the stock. Since September of last year, massive uncertainty had been priced into the stock and this program, and as a knock-on result, upcoming regulatory discussions were being seen more as a negative catalyst. So with this announcement, the stock and the overall program are getting materially de-risked. It also keeps MoonLake on track for a BLA submission in 2H26, which will be a massive momentum builder for shares. The outcome also strengthens the overall confidence in sonelokimab’s clinical package from a regulatory viewpoint, and I think management is now much more confident in their regulatory strategy.

A word on the TAM

Now, a big part of the investor buy-in for MoonLake shares in 2025 was the potential for sonelokimab. If we take just the HS treatment application, the overall market is currently in a growth phase from a combination of rising diagnoses, greater awareness, and expanded use of biologics as a treatment option. Across the major global markets, the HS market was estimated at around $1.8 to $1.9 billion back in 2024 and is projected to expand significantly over the next decade, with some estimates ranging up to about $7.8 billion by 2034.

Now, within this broader opportunity, biologic therapies for moderate‑to‑severe HS are a pretty key commercial segment just by themselves. We’ve only seen a few approved products so far in AbbVie’s Humira/adalimumab and Novartis’s Cosentyx/secukinumab. In some regions, UCB’s Bimzelx/bimekizumab is in use. Humira, if you remember, used to be pretty blockbuster in autoimmune disease overall. Back in 2024, they saw around $9 billion in total annual sales across all indications, which actually only accounts for a fraction of that sales sum in HS. The HS segment itself is pretty niche within the larger immunology franchise. Cosentyx’s broader sales brought in about $1.7 billion globally, which signals a strong uptake for IL‑17 pathway targeting therapies but is shared across indications. Lastly, Bimzelx has seen pretty rapid revenue growth in its first years on market, with figures approaching the €800 million mark in net sales, which goes to show an adoption in HS and other IL‑17‑responsive diseases.

Now, given this backdrop, I think sonelokimab’s TAM in HS could meaningfully exceed $1 billion annually if eventually approved and positioned competitively by management. Especially if we see it placed as multiple biologics and next-gen therapy with expanding treatment uptake and patient eligibility.

Risk, no guarantee of approval

We have to be very careful here not to confuse the most recent FDA response of not needing additional studies with an increased probability of approval later down the line. So even though the recent Type B meeting was positive with the FDA, there is no guarantee that sonelokimab will ultimately be approved for HS. All this indicates is that the FDA feels MoonLake can submit a BLA using existing MIRA, VELA-1, and VELA-2 data without additional trials. And the positive reaction from the market is from the BLA coming much sooner than they expected, with no need for an additional spend, not because they think the FDA likes sonelokimab’s chances.

The FDA may actually still require further clarification, such as labelling restrictions, or even identify safety or statistical concerns throughout the review at a later stage. Additionally, we should keep in mind that the VELA-2 narrowly missed statistical significance on its primary composite endpoint due to the high placebo response we saw, which could influence regulatory interpretation of effectiveness later. Therefore, although the Type B feedback reduces regulatory uncertainty just around trial requirements, it, in my opinion, does not materially increase the probability of a positive FDA decision. Investors still remain exposed to the standard clinical and regulatory risks inherent to any first-in-class therapeutics.

Valuation

If you wanted to pick up MoonLake shares in 1H25, you would have had to pay up, especially in late summer when the stock was approaching the $60 mark. Now, a lot has happened since then, and a lot has been priced out. Shares have been traded on very positive momentum behind sonelokimab until it was priced out on September 28th. As a result, shares are significantly cheaper now and are trying to recover, going into a potential BLA this year. Since the 29th of September, the day after the bad news, shares have recovered 129% and can now be picked up in the mid-$14 range with a far more attractive valuation profile.

MoonLake now has a market cap of about $805.8 million and a cash sum of $380.5 million. It gives them a pretty solid liquidity buffer relative to its enterprise value of $503.3 million. Now, traditional valuation metrics like price-to-earnings and EV-to-EBITDA are, of course, not meaningful at this stage since the company is in a pre-revenue and clinical development stage. However, its price-to-book ratio of 2.49x tells me the market values it only modestly above its net asset base, so there's a lot of investor optimism tied primarily to sonelokimab’s upcoming BLA submission. In terms of volume, MoonLake’s stock typically trades with average daily volume in the range of 2 million to 2.6 million shares, so there is solid liquidity here for a clinical‑stage biotech name.

Looking ahead

Going forward, I like MoonLake's stock momentum so far and feel there is a lot more upside with the BLA coming in 2H26. There's a long way to go until then, and with the news that an extra study isn't needed, I think management will boast an improved cash position come next earnings. Earnings are actually set to fall sometime around February 26th, so the pathway for sonelokimab should be a lot clearer then.

data.title: Adobe: I'm Pounding The Table On This Unloved Compounder (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I’m such a huge fan of the new year.

Aside from the dramatic increase in family time that most of us get, it’s a great time to stop, rest, unplug, and look around at the world for a while.

I often find that when I come back in January, I’m filled with all sorts of new ideas, many of which are investment theses that have been percolating—both consciously and unconsciously—during the time off.

One of the most interesting ideas that I’m struggling to shake off recently has been Adobe Inc. (ADBE), the number one maker of creative/design software products.

Once upon a time, the stock was a market darling, trading with a double-digit price-to-sales ratio and a P/E above 100. From the company’s initial public offering in 1987 through late 2021, the stock produced incredible gains of more than 350,000%, turning early investors into millionaires:

Throughout this growth, and especially in the 2010s, investors routinely praised Adobe’s strong revenue growth, recurring revenue business model, and strong operating margins. It was the definition of a compounder.

Fast forward to today, and by many metrics, Adobe is still the same business.

Growth has slowed somewhat, although the company’s top-line sales are still growing at double-digit rates. Not only that, but the company’s strong recurring revenue business model has become the status quo in the software industry. Margins also remain strong, and profit growth has been sound.

The only big thing that has changed—apparently—is the market's perception of the company, which has deeply soured. From the stock’s all-time high near $700, shares currently trade at roughly $335, less than half that total.

As generative AI tools have advanced rapidly over the last three years, it appears as though investors have become increasingly concerned about Adobe’s position as design-focused startups like Figma, Inc. (FIG) and other tools gobble up user interest and market share.

Obviously, changes in the market for design tools are important and shouldn’t be dismissed by investors. That said, I believe the current valuation—at only 19 times earnings—dramatically undervalues ADBE's high-quality business.

Previously, I've covered ADBE, and my most recent rating was a Buy—largely on the back of the same trends I'll mention in this article. However, with the valuation looking even more depressed and the company's progress on AI advancing even further, I think the stock looks even more attractive now. Hence the upgrade.

Today, I’ll outline what makes Adobe such a true compounder, address fears around competition from Figma/AI startups, and make the case that investors should strongly consider buying into Adobe’s long-term compounding story at this price.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

What Makes A Compounder?

Before explaining just why I like Adobe so much, it’s probably worth outlining what I mean when I say 'compounder', as analysts across the market define it in different ways. A compounder, for me, simply refers to a stock that I believe has the potential to outperform the S&P 500 for a longer-than-average period of time.

That is, when you invest in a stock that has compound qualities, your investment builds on itself at a faster-than-average rate, producing meaningfully higher long-run average returns.

When it comes to long-term investing, I consider compound stocks the holy grail.

In terms of finding compounder stocks, I view it as a matter of assessing two things correctly: supply and demand. Meaning - is demand for the company’s shares increasing over time? Similarly, is the supply of shares set to fall over time?

When you have a strong track record of increasing demand for—and a decreasing supply of—shares, price has only one way to go: up.

For me, I look at a few key financial metrics to determine whether or not a company’s track record is compounder-esque.

On the demand side, I look for a long track record of steadily growing revenues and net income. Growing revenue is important because it highlights a company’s ability to grow the overall scope and scale of its business. Profitability matters because the business must have a feasible, valuable business model.

Many companies have grown revenues quickly but have been unable to figure out their business model, leaving investors in tatters.

On the supply side, I like to see a company that is actively returning cash to shareholders in the form of buybacks. For a company’s share count to shrink, there needs to be leftover free cash flow to buy back shares in the market. As a result, I track shares outstanding to see what management’s attitude is toward equity investors.

When you have a company growing revenues and profits while shrinking the outstanding share count, it can be an incredibly powerful engine for stock appreciation.

Adobe As A Compounder

I see Adobe as an incredibly high-quality company because it fits neatly into this definition.

Over the last five years, Adobe has grown revenues from $12.9 billion to $23.8 billion, representing a compounded annual growth rate of more than 13%:

This is incredibly strong growth at an incredibly predictable and stable rate. Like Adobe or not, the company has an excellent track record when it comes to growing the business, providing value, and monetizing its customers.

On the profit side, the company has also done well, growing net profit from $2.95 billion in 2020 to $7.13 billion over the last twelve months, representing a compounded annual growth rate of nearly 16%:

For the profit growth measurement, I took a slightly longer view because the COVID shock actually pulled forward a considerable amount of demand for the company's products, thus making the post-2021 period have a slightly lower growth rate.

Overall, though, the growth rates here are fantastic, and it appears as though demand for the company’s shares is moving up and to the right.

Similarly, on the supply side, Adobe has taken its profits and distributed them in a meaningful way to shareholders, reducing the outstanding share count from 484 million to 417 million over the last five years:

This is an incredible 14% reduction in just five short years, which is something you don’t see very often. Plus, the recent collapse in the share price has meant meaningful progress on this front in recent quarters.

Taken together, Adobe is positioned to deliver incredible ongoing returns as a result of its business track record, solid growth, and disciplined capital return strategy.

Risks & Why Now?

Unfortunately, as I outlined at the beginning, Adobe’s stock has fallen by more than half since late 2021 as slowing revenue growth and an explosion of generative AI tools have weighed on investor sentiment.

Revenue growth has slowed somewhat in recent years, and in the most recent quarter, Adobe’s year-over-year revenue growth was only about 10.5%:

Compared to the 2010s, this is much slower growth than investors are used to. This, in turn, has led to significant multiple contraction.

At the same time, Adobe faces more competitive threats than ever. Figma, a popular web-based design tool, recently went public and has a fast-growing user base of Adobe's ICPs.

Similarly, new gen AI tools like Midjourney, Sora, and Nano Banana call into question Adobe’s ability to innovate. Sure, the company has released Firefly, its cross-platform generative AI model, but speaking anecdotally, many users in the space don’t see Adobe as being in the lead with this incredibly important technology.

As a result, Adobe stock has re-rated from its average range of the last five years. Between 2021 and 2025, Adobe stock traded between 7x and 21x sales and a P/E of 26x and 65x.

These are huge ranges, yes, but the stock has historically been priced at a premium.

As of the most recent quarter, shares of Adobe currently trade at 19.8x earnings and 5.8x sales:

This is the cheapest Adobe shares have been since the early 2010s, when the market was coming out of the Great Recession:

Simply put, I believe Adobe stock is a strong buy for two core reasons.

First, the price is cheaper than both its own history, peers and the broader market. Shares of Adobe look incredibly well priced for long-term growth and multiple expansion.

Secondly—and perhaps more importantly—I believe that Adobe is reacting quicker than investors currently think when it comes to incorporating the best of Figma and gen AI tools into its design/creative products.

On the recent earnings call, management went to great lengths to highlight just how much success the company is having with new AI tools and workflows, which should ultimately drive strong value for users (emphasis mine):

We are leveraging AI to accelerate innovation to anticipate and serve the needs of large and expanding audiences...

With the Firefly application, we are giving Creators a rich set of generative AI capabilities that allow users to generate with Adobe and partner models, ideate with Firefly Boards, and create and edit videos and images through conversational experiences blended with direct manipulation. Along with the Firefly app, we've introduced new products like Premiere mobile with YouTube integration and Photoshop mobile to further serve Creators anywhere they create.

[Recent] highlights include: Establishing ourselves within leading AI ecosystems with partnerships and integrations across AWS, Azure, Google Gemini, HUMAIN, Microsoft Copilot, OpenAI and others, offering unprecedented access, choice and flexibility to customers; leading the way in AI innovation around conversational and agentic interfaces, including products like LLM Optimizer and Acrobat AI Assistant, creating custom models through Firefly Services, Firefly Foundry and GenStudio as well as infusing AI within our applications including Photoshop, Lightroom, Express and Premiere... driving significant new user acquisition across individuals and enterprises with products, including Acrobat, Creative Cloud, Express and Firefly achieving total MAU growth of greater than 15% year-over-year.

As a result, over the long haul, I’m not expecting significant subscriber bleed or market share loss for Adobe.

Additionally, the company’s size, scale, and client base still position it to deliver compliant gen AI work product, something that other models can’t yet boast. In other words, Nano Banana is great for making memes, but when it comes to professional marketing, ad spend, creative work, etc., Adobe is still the king.

Summary

As a result, I view Adobe as a 'Strong Buy' compounder that investors shouldn’t overlook.

Sure, growth has slowed a bit, and increased competition from Figma and gen AI tools has made investors jittery, but the company is responding, profits are up, and I expect the company’s excellent long-term track record to continue into the future.

Thus, my rating.

Cheers~

data.title: 2025 Market Prediction Competition: The Winners
data.description:
In December 2024, Seeking Alpha invited analysts to participate in a 2025 Market Prediction competition. We received 23 eligible articles. All entries were submitted by the December 21, 2024 deadline. Here's a look at all the winners who had the 3 most accurate S&P 500 predictions (for reference, the S&P 500 closed 2025 at 6,845).

1) Wavelength Research – S&P 500 In 2025: Trump Vs. AI Vs. Fed - prediction 6,820

2) Daniel Jones – Get Ready For New Stock Market Records In 2025 - prediction 6,750

3) Ricardo Fernandez – S&P 500: Overshoot In Q1 2025 And Ending With Double-Digit Upside Potential - prediction 6,700

First place receives an award of US $1,500, second place US $1,000 and third place US $500.

Thank you to everyone who participated!

data.title: Northrop Grumman: Be Careful Chasing The $1.7 Trillion Defense Budget News
data.description:
Introduction

In my last article covering major defense contractor Northrop Grumman Corp. (NOC), I opened with an assessment of the massive U.S. deficit and the austerity measures announced by President Trump, including Elon Musk's involvement, which were still the subject of much debate at the time.

President Trump announced yesterday that he would seek to increase the defense budget for 2027 to a whopping $1.5 trillion, making a moderation in U.S. government spending an increasingly unlikely expectation. From the perspective of shareholders in prime defense contractors, such a massive increase in defense spending would be naturally good news even if tied to certain conditions, so it was not surprising that shares of NOC and its peers jumped in early trading yesterday.

In this update, I will share my assessment of the latest news, particularly regarding Northrop Grumman's major projects, and explain why I have not changed my previous conclusions. However, I will also discuss the bigger picture, considering my broad exposure to the U.S. defense sector, with positions at Lockheed Martin Corp. (LMT), RTX Corp. (RTX), L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX), and General Dynamics Corp. (GD) in addition to Northrop Grumman.

The $1.5 Trillion Mega Defense Budget – Really a Major Tailwind for Northrop Grumman and the U.S. Defense Sector?

One of the main reasons why I like Northrop Grumman despite its concentration on U.S. defense programs (unlike competitors with commercial aviation exposure such as RTX or General Dynamics) is its focus on very highly prioritized and prestigious programs with expected contractual cash flows over several decades. As I explained in detail in my first bullish note on Northrop Grumman back in mid-2024, it is primarily the B-21 Raider program and the LGM-35A Sentinel program that (despite some setbacks) are likely to make the largest contribution to Northrop's future cash flow growth. It is also important to note that Northrop is likely to benefit significantly from the increasing introduction of space-related military programs.

Given the high U.S. deficit, it naturally seems particularly risky to own shares in a prime defense contractor that focuses on only a few large projects that are still in the early stages of their life cycle. For this reason, I also own stock in L3Harris Technologies, a much more broadly diversified (but also smaller) company than Northrop, which is also likely to benefit significantly from the expected growth in space-related defense and warfare. What I particularly like about L3Harris is its involvement in many core domains of defense (most recently also rocket motors), albeit less directly than Northrop and also Lockheed Martin (with its F-35 fighter jet franchise).

At the same time, I believe it is important to highlight the prestigious nature of the projects awarded to Northrop, which is the main reason why I remain optimistic about the company's future, leaving aside the $1.5 trillion proposal for a moment. Given Trump's "The Golden Dome for America" initiative, the development of an integrated air and missile defense system, it seems highly unrealistic that the same administration would cut spending on projects such as the LGM-35A Sentinel. After all, it is supposed to replace all 450 Minuteman III missiles starting in 2029. The LGM-30, which came into service already in 1970, is currently the only land-based intercontinental ballistic missile system in service in the U.S. Of course, it is possible that ballistic missiles launched from submarines could gain further strategic importance, but I would not underestimate the visible geopolitical power exuded by the LGM-35A, which is currently expected to remain in service until 2075. The same considerations naturally also apply to the B-21 Raider program.

Clearly, defense contractors directly involved in prestigious large-scale projects such as Northrop Grumman would benefit most if the proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget is implemented. Furthermore, cost overruns are likely to be less problematic going forward, at least from a political perspective (recall that the LGM-35A program incurred a critical breach per the Nunn-McCurdy Act in 2024).

The proposed $1.5 trillion represents a 76% increase over the $850 billion approved for 2025 (see p. 65 of the official budget documentation), raising the question of how an already highly indebted country intends to finance this increase. The market is clearly already skeptical, as yesterday's extremely short-lived rally showed. NOC shares rose more than 10% in early trading yesterday from Wednesday's closing price of $577.01 but fell back during the day to close just above $590, a gain of just over 2% for the day. In fact, NOC shares are currently trading at levels last seen in August 2025, suggesting that yesterday's news is unlikely to have a significant positive impact on Northrop's future earnings going forward.

At the same time, the brief decline in defense stocks on Wednesday following Trump's comments on banning dividend payments and share buybacks under certain circumstances shows how strongly the market is currently influenced by news. Such a volatile environment is, of course, ideal for enhancing one's returns by selling options, but it is nonetheless crucial to stick to the long-term fundamentals, which, in my view, do not exactly argue for a massive increase in defense spending.

U.S. defense consumption expenditures and gross investments rose 5.2% year-over-year to $1.162 trillion in the third quarter of 2025 on a seasonally adjusted annual basis (the most recent figure; see item 55 on page 10 of this document from the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis). Consumption expenditures accounted for 78.6% of total spending, which is consistent with past figures but nonetheless a very significant percentage (Figure 1). In my view, any further significant increase in the defense budget should be aimed at strengthening investment. However, if the past is a meaningful indicator of the future, this will not be the case. Furthermore, I am skeptical that the proposed massive increase in the defense budget will meet with broad acceptance, as defense spending has already risen sharply in recent years, as shown by the percentage values above the blue/red bars in Figure 1. A further intensification of geopolitical

Conversely, one could argue that U.S. defense-related consumption expenditures and gross investments account for an increasingly smaller share of gross domestic product (Figure 2), thereby justifying the proposed massive increase from the perspective of balanced allocation.

However, long-term interest rates remain sticky at elevated levels (Figure 3), and the government is currently focusing on short-term refinancing. Interest payments have consequently exploded, and so far it looks like the sky is the limit (Figure 4). Against this backdrop, I find it extremely difficult to believe that the proposal for an above-normal increase in the defense budget has any significant chance of being approved - unless, of course, new instruments are employed to get the increasingly unsustainable national debt, which now stands at more than $38 trillion, under control.

In order to better manage debt, stablecoins could indeed be an option. At least in theory, minting a platinum coin with an extremely high face value could be another option. This concept, which dates back to presidential candidate Bo Gritz in the early 1990s but only became widely discussed during the 2011 debt ceiling crisis, is legally permissible in principle (see 31 U.S. Code § 5112 – "Denominations, specifications, and design of coins", lit. k), but of course would have serious implications for the international acceptance of U.S. government bonds and, above all, for inflation.

Conversely, we must not forget the financial health of other economies, such as large parts of the European Union or Japan (although the latter has the advantage of most of its debt being held domestically). Overall, I therefore view the current global situation with the analogy of someone running from an aggressive bear. It is not necessary to be faster than the bear, but it is enough to be able to outrun others who are also fleeing from the bear. In this context, the latest rhetoric regarding an aggressive increase in defense spending, the intervention in Venezuela, and, of course, ambitions with Greenland could be interpreted as an attempt by the current administration to demonstrate that it is still the world's leading superpower.

Conclusion

Overall, Trump's proposal to increase defense spending from $850 billion in 2025 to $1.5 trillion in 2027 sounds like a massive tailwind for the entire U.S. defense sector, and especially for prime contractors with extremely long-term and prestigious projects such as Northrop Grumman.

However, given the tense financial situation in the U.S. – particularly regarding interest payments – the question arises as to how this increase of almost 80% is to be financed. Given this uncertainty, it is not surprising that yesterday's rally was short-lived. Investors should also view yesterday morning's trading activity in the context of President Trump's Executive Order signed the day before, which aims to penalize defense companies that "fail to invest their own capital in production capacity insufficiently prioritize U.S. government contracts" by restricting or prohibiting them from buying back shares or paying dividends. Overall, I view yesterday's rally as more of a relief rally than as a sign of an immediate acceptance of the proposed increase in defense spending.

Focusing less on the current extremely news-driven market and more on Northrop Grumman's fundamentals, I believe the company remains very well positioned for the long term. I consider it highly unlikely that either of its two flagship programs (B-21 Raider and LGM-35A Sentinel) are threatened by significant budget cuts or outright cancellation, primarily due to their prestigious nature and strategic importance. In my previous article, I estimated the fair value of NOC stock at $520 based on a cost of equity of 7% and relatively conservative long-term growth expectations, a price at which I'd feel very comfortable adding additional NOC shares to my position.

If both the LGM-35A Sentinel and B-21 Raider programs prove to be reliable sources of significant contractual cash flows with good long-term growth, and of course assuming there are no significant recalls, delays, or other setbacks, I expect a significantly higher fair value from a long-term perspective and thus continued solid returns for shareholders, mainly through capital appreciation. At the same time, I maintain my thesis that Northrop Grumman remains similarly interesting from a dividend perspective thanks to its continued healthy dividend growth (Figure 5). Although the starting dividend yield is comparatively low, the reliable cash flows associated with Northrop's extremely long-term projects should continue to enable meaningful dividend increases.

That said, I see no reason to increase my fair value estimate against the backdrop of the proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget for 2027. At the same time, Trump's latest comments reinforce my previous expectation that a flat defense budget or an outright cut to reduce the deficit is an unrealistically negative expectation. Therefore, I continue to sleep well with my positions in NOC, RTX, LMT, LHX, and GD, however, without directly participating in this currently very news-driven market. At the same time, I would like to add that my direct exposure to the U.S. defense sector accounts for only 7.4% of the current value of my stock portfolio, with my target allocation being around 10%.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: America's Invasion Could Be Your Persuasion To Invest In This Dividend Aristocrat
data.description:
Introduction

With the U.S. capture of the Venezuelan President, Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) jumped on the news, likely due to the country's oil reserves. And President Trump mentioned that oil companies will invest heavily to rebuild the country.

He has also stated that the U.S. will maintain control until a safe, proper, and judicious transition happens.

When that will be? No one knows. However, the Energy (XLRE) sector will likely do well since Trump stated the capture of the Venezuelan President will allow American based companies to drill in the country's long neglected oil reserves.

Going forward, companies like XOM should see solid growth as a result. Currently, peer Chevron (CVX) is the only one with operations in the country.

And if you're a long-term dividend investor, then XOM could potentially be attractive as this could lead to higher growth. Thus, positively impacting their share price.

In this article, I discuss Exxon Mobil's fundamentals, dividend history, and why this Dividend Aristocrat could be an attractive investment for long-term, income-oriented investors.

Previous Thesis

It's been more than 2 years since I last covered Exxon Mobil, last assigning the stock a buy rating back in October 2023. Since, the stock is up, but has significantly lagged the performance of the S&P (SP500).

XOM is up close to 19% in the past 27 months, while the index is up close to 67%. Despite their underperformance, I believed XOM was attractive due to their strong fundamentals and upside to their price target of $128. Currently, the stock sits slightly below this at $125.36 a share.

Latest Earnings

During their latest Q3 earnings this past October, XOM delivered what some may view as a disappointing earnings report. Their top and bottom lines fell year-over-year due to lower crude-oil prices, suppressed margins, inflation, and lower volumes.

Through the first 3 quarters, both revenue and earnings per share were down from the prior year. Revenue totaled $249.9 billion, down from $266.2 billion, while their bottom line fell double-digits to $5.16 from $6.12.

However, the oil giant saw some positives during the quarter. Guyana, which now accounts for 2/3 of the company's workforce, saw more than 700,000 oil barrels per day.

XOM also launched a new development in Guyana, Yellowtail, which contributed to the record 700,000 barrels per day. Oil & gas production increased more than 139,000 barrels a day.

The Permian Basin saw a production record close to 1.7 million barrels per day. Additionally, XOM acquired 80 acres of land, increasing drilling capacity, leading to potentially higher returns going forward.

Although some projects like Yellowtail have already come online, XOM has a slew of projects in the coming years that will likely drive solid growth for the foreseeable future.

Here are of their upcoming projects in their Upstream segment.

Here are some of their Energy, Chemical, & Specialty Products projects.

And lastly, Low Carbon Solutions projects.

In Q3, aside from Energy, profits fell in each of their segments. Energy's performance was driven by higher volumes due to increased refinery output and projects.

Upstream, their largest, saw profits fall due to lower realizations and higher expenses.

Both the Chemical & Specialty Products segments were impacted by higher costs, which also impacted margins.

But management expects improvements to be driven by cost-savings initiatives, growth from current and future projects, improved efficiencies, and technological innovation.

Recent Dividend Increase

In early November, Exxon Mobil raised its dividend 4% to $1.03. This was a similar increase to last year, when the company increased the dividend by 4%. While they had exhibited strong cash flow growth despite volatility in their segments, some investors may have been disappointed in the 4% increase.

Management addressed why the dividend growth rate hasn't been more competitive vs the broader market during Q3:

We look at our overall dividend growth rate, and we constantly think about sustainability, right? We think about competitiveness, we think about growth. And when we measure it on those 3 pretty important qualitative factors, we feel pretty happy with where we've ended up.

The increase marked 43 consecutive years of dividend increases. XOM also has a solid dividend yield above 3% currently. Despite cash from operations declining year-over-year from $42.8 billion to $39.3 billion, the company only paid $12.9 billion in dividends through the first 3 quarters.

Furthermore, management raised their cash flow & earnings outlook through 2030. This should instill some confidence in their dividend sustainability going forward. And could also see management conduct slightly higher dividend raises in the 6% to 8% range going forward.

Because of XOM's strong business, projects, and bright outlook due to Venezuela's heavier crude vs America's lighter crude, I have no doubt that Exxon Mobil will join the Dividend King's list in the next 7 years.

Buybacks & Balance Sheet

One reason I believe XOM's management is more conservative on their dividend growth is that the company prefers to buyback shares, returning capital to shareholders this way.

During the quarter, XOM returned $9.4 billion. Of this, $5.1 billion was share repurchases. And for the fiscal year, management expects to repurchase $20 billion, slightly higher than the $19 billion in 2024.

As a result of XOM's strong balance sheet, this not only gives them flexibility for future acquisitions, it also enables them to buyback a substantial amount of shares. Which, going forward, could lead to solid earnings growth.

As far as their balance sheet goes, XOM's was in good shape with no near-term debt maturing, an A credit rating, and only $32.8 billion in long-term debt. This was minimal in comparison to their market cap of over $500 billion.

Liquidity was robust with $13.9 billion in cash & cash equivalents. Their balance sheet enables them to have the flexibility to make acquisitions, growing the business. In Q3, acquisitions totaled $2.4 billion. And going forward, I expect more in the years ahead.

Venezuela Could Unlock More Growth

Using their end-of-year earnings estimate, the company's forward P/E sits at 18x. This is higher than both their 5-year average and sector median. As evidenced by their quality, I believe XOM deserves to trade at a premium.

Despite their valuation, XOM's forward multiple still sits well-below peer Chevron's 22.29x at the time of writing. Shell (SHEL) and BP (BP) both trade significantly lower at 11.43x and 12.24x, respectively.

But as previously mentioned, with America's taking control of the country with the largest oil reserves, and the President mentioning the plan to have U.S. companies rebuild the country's energy infrastructure. If so, this could result in long-term growth for XOM and peers alike if successful.

If so, XOM's investment case is compelling as higher earnings growth will likely lead to higher dividend and share price growth. By 2028, the oil giant offers solid upside, according to FastGraphs. But if U.S. companies successfully reinvest in Venezuela, I suspect this could be even higher, closer to the $150 - $160 level.

Risks & Conclusion

Key risks for XOM include continued volatility in U.S. oil & gas prices. Moreover, geopolitical tensions will also play a part, as what will happen with cooperation from Venezuela remains unknown. Especially, since the President's military action against the country could face scrutiny.

However, with oil prices currently down, I believe Exxon's long-term investment case is compelling. JPMorgan (JPM) also named them as a top income investment idea for 2026.

While shares could remain volatile in the near to medium term, the invasion in Venezuela could unlock value for XOM, leading to strong growth going forward. This, their solid dividend yield above 3%, upside potential, and likelihood to see higher growth make them a compelling long-term investment.

data.title: Stride: Time To Double Down
data.description:
I Keep My Bullish Stance on LRN Stock

I initiated my coverage of Stride, Inc. (LRN) stock in early November, a few days after its massive drop after its dramatic drop in price following the Q1 data and commentary.
